entry.title: GR Based IV not allowed in return STO
entry.content: System pops up error message 'Message No. MEPO025 Indicator for GR-based invoice verification used not allowed' when creating inter-company return STO, and Incoterms defaulted from PIR is removed at return STO item level.

entry.title: Re: Clarification on Personalization Migration from SAP F...
entry.content:
Hello,

Fiori 2 → Fiori 3 User Personalization Migration

What is Retained
Personalization                        Retained?
Theme                                              Yes
User Defaults                                 Yes
Favorites                                      With conditions
Tile Order / Groups Layout      Not automatic

Migration Options

My Home Import (Post-2023 FPS01): First login to My Home shows a message strip → click "Import Apps Now" — one-time action only.
/UIF/MIGRATE_FES_PERSO: Transport-based migration across clients/systems (SAP Note 2789848).
/UI2/FLP_GTP: Groups → Pages content migration (role level, not user personalization level).

Please note that SAP does not provide a mass migration tool for user personalization from Groups to Spaces & Pages.

Following SAP KBA for your reference as well:
3448332 Documentation for Setting Up My Home in SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01
3603720 Copying Fiori Home Page Personalization (Export/Import via My Home Advanced tab)
3436068 Fiori migration from Classic to Spaces and Pages Mode Info on Importing Personalization
3322675 Guidelines on Migrating Fiori Launchpad from Groups to Spaces & Pages
3368323 How to import Apps and Personalizations from Classic Home Page to My Home (Post S/4HANA 2022)
2789848 Migration report for homepage and SAPUI5 application personalization (/UIF/MIGRATE_FES_PERSO)

Best regards,
Joe

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud - Automatic Printing of GR Label
entry.content: I managed to get it working. For anyone wondering, reference SAP Note 3669809.

entry.title: AADSTS90072 when accessing newly assigned SAP Practice System after previous system expired
entry.content:
Hi everyone,
I am experiencing an access issue with an SAP Learning Practice System and would appreciate any guidance.

Issue:
When I access the practice system URL:

https://my414802.s4hana.cloud.sap/ui

I am redirected to Microsoft Entra ID authentication and receive the following error:

AADSTS90072

User account 'xxxxx@xxx.xx.jp' from identity provider
'https://sts.windows.net/.... -0932264d1fd5/'
does not exist in tenant 'SAP SE' and cannot access the application
'SAPID UnifiedAuthentication PROD MSEC' in that tenant.

The account needs to be added as an external user in the tenant first.

※I am using my corporate Microsoft account.

Has anyone encountered AADSTS90072 in SAP Learning Practice System environment, and if so, how was it resolved?

Any advice would be greatly appreciated.
Thank you.

entry.title: In-System Notifications for Executive Review Users upon Worksheet Launch
entry.content:
Hello colleagues,

I have a question regarding In-System Notifications for Executive Review Users:

Is there a built-in way to notify users directly from within the system that compensation worksheets are launched and their Executive Review forms are ready to access?

Specifically, when we want to notify users who have access to the forms only via the Executive Review functionality (for those, who are not directly included in approval route steps). Can this be handled in-system, or do we need to notify this specific group manually outside of the system (e.g., via standard email, Teams, etc.)?

entry.title: Request Vendor & Customer Payment Receipt Advice Print Automatic Email
entry.content:

entry.title: Goods issue date calculation after PO creation
entry.content:
For the POs user have to anticipate the goods issue date by one day. If user advance the delivery date one day, the issue date is brought forward by 2. Below are the PO's being affected

4577918278 

4577918274
4577918276
4577918278
4577918280
4577918282 

Please help us to understand how the goods issue date being calculated after the PO creation as per the delivery date.

User is expecting it should update delivery date = good issue date + 2 in this manner. Please suggest what settings we can do to achieve these combination

entry.title: Context Engineering in SAP Analytics Cloud: Building ContextWallet for AI and Genie Integrations
entry.content:
Introduction

 

 

 

Over the last few years, the conversation around analytics has shifted dramatically. Traditional dashboards are no longer viewed as the final destination for business intelligence. Instead, organizations are increasingly exploring how Artificial Intelligence can work alongside analytics platforms to provide conversational insights, automated explanations, and faster decision-making.

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is already a powerful platform for planning, reporting, and analytics. At the same time, AI-powered solutions such as Databricks Genie and Large Language Models (LLMs) are enabling users to interact with data using natural language rather than traditional reports and dashboards.

However, while experimenting with SAC and AI integrations, I encountered a challenge that is rarely discussed but becomes immediately apparent once you start building enterprise AI solutions.

The challenge is not generating answers.

The challenge is providing the AI with the right business context.

Imagine a finance analyst working inside a SAC dashboard. Before analyzing any KPI, the analyst typically applies several business filters such as:

  • Company Code

  • Fiscal Year

  • Version

  • Currency

  • Profit Center

  • Account Type

These selections define the meaning of every chart, table, and KPI displayed on the dashboard. In many cases, the same metric can produce completely different interpretations depending on the selected filters.

Now consider a scenario where the analyst asks an AI assistant:

Why is EBITDA decreasing?

For a human looking at the dashboard, the question is obvious because the context is already visible on the screen. The user knows which company, fiscal year, and reporting version are currently selected.

For the AI, however, the question is incomplete.

Without access to the dashboard context, the AI does not know:

  • Which company is being analyzed

  • Which fiscal year is selected

  • Which reporting version is active

  • Which currency is being used

  • Which business entity is currently in focus

As a result, organizations often face a disconnect between analytics systems and AI systems. The dashboard contains the business context, while the AI receives only the question.

This observation led me to think about a concept that I call Context Engineering.

Just as Prompt Engineering focuses on designing effective instructions for AI systems, Context Engineering focuses on ensuring that AI systems receive the right business context before generating a response.

In enterprise analytics, context is often more important than the question itself. A perfectly crafted prompt can still produce an inaccurate answer if the AI lacks awareness of the user's current analytical context.

To explore this idea further, I started building a custom SAP Analytics Cloud widget called ContextWallet.

The goal of ContextWallet is simple:

Capture, preserve, and share dashboard context across SAC applications, pages, and AI systems.

Instead of repeatedly passing filters between dashboards, scripts, and external AI services, ContextWallet acts as a centralized context layer that can store business selections, maintain state across pages, create reusable snapshots, and prepare structured context payloads for AI assistants such as Databricks Genie.

While ContextWallet began as an experiment, it quickly evolved into something more interesting: a practical implementation of Context Engineering within SAP Analytics Cloud.

In the following sections, I will discuss the motivation behind this approach, the architecture of ContextWallet, and how it can help bridge the gap between traditional analytics and AI-powered decision-making.

The Missing Piece: Why Prompt Engineering Alone Is Not Enough

 

 

 

When discussing Generative AI, one concept appears almost everywhere: Prompt Engineering.

Prompt Engineering focuses on designing better instructions for AI systems in order to obtain more accurate, relevant, and useful responses. As Large Language Models became more capable, organizations quickly realized that the quality of the prompt significantly influences the quality of the response.

For example, instead of asking:

Explain revenue performance.

A more effective prompt might be:

Explain the revenue performance for Company Code 1000 in Fiscal Year 2025 and identify the top three contributing factors.

The second prompt provides more information and therefore allows the AI to generate a more focused response.

However, while experimenting with AI-driven analytics scenarios, I realized that prompt quality is only one part of the problem.

Even the most carefully designed prompt can fail if the AI lacks awareness of the user's current business context.

Consider a typical SAP Analytics Cloud dashboard used by a finance team.

A user may already have selected:

  • Company Code = 1000

  • Fiscal Year = 2025

  • Version = Actual

  • Currency = USD

  • Entity = EU_Corp

The dashboard understands these selections perfectly. Every chart, table, KPI, and calculation is already operating within this context.

Now the user asks:

Why is revenue decreasing?

From a human perspective, the question is completely valid because the context is already visible on the screen.

From the AI's perspective, however, the question is ambiguous.

Revenue for which company?

Which fiscal year?

Which version?

Which entity?

Without access to the dashboard context, the AI may either produce a generic answer or require the user to repeat information that already exists within the application.

This creates an inefficient user experience.

The user already provided the context through filters and selections, yet the AI remains unaware of it.

This is where I believe a new discipline becomes necessary.

Introducing Context Engineering

If Prompt Engineering is about optimizing the question, Context Engineering is about optimizing the information surrounding the question.

Context Engineering focuses on ensuring that AI systems receive the right business context before generating a response.

In analytics applications, context may include:

  • Active dashboard filters

  • User selections

  • Input control values

  • Reporting periods

  • Organizational hierarchies

  • Planning versions

  • Saved dashboard states

  • Business entities

Rather than repeatedly asking users to provide this information, the application should be capable of capturing and managing it automatically.

In other words, before asking:

Why is EBITDA decreasing?

the system should already know:

Company Code: 1000
Fiscal Year: 2025
Version: Actual
Currency: USD
Profit Center: PC001

This transforms AI interactions from generic conversations into context-aware business discussions.

The Role of Context in Enterprise AI

Unlike public AI use cases, enterprise AI systems rarely operate in isolation.

They exist within business applications, planning systems, reporting platforms, and analytical environments.

As a result, context often becomes more valuable than the prompt itself.

A simple question with rich business context can generate a highly relevant response.

Conversely, a sophisticated prompt with no context may generate a vague or inaccurate answer.

This realization became the foundation for ContextWallet.

Rather than treating context as something temporary and scattered across filters, pages, and widgets, I wanted to create a centralized context layer that could capture, preserve, and share business context across SAP Analytics Cloud applications and future AI integrations.

The objective was not simply to build another utility widget.

The objective was to experiment with Context Engineering as a practical architectural pattern for AI-powered analytics.

Introducing ContextWallet

ContextWallet

 

After identifying the need for a dedicated context layer within SAP Analytics Cloud, I began exploring possible implementation approaches.

The primary objective was not to create another utility widget or temporary storage mechanism. Instead, I wanted to build a reusable framework that could act as a centralized repository for business context and eventually serve as a bridge between SAC dashboards and AI-powered analytics systems.

This idea became ContextWallet.

ContextWallet is a custom SAP Analytics Cloud widget designed to capture, preserve, manage, and share business context across dashboard pages, applications, and external AI integrations.

At its core, ContextWallet acts as a lightweight context management engine that stores the current analytical state of an application in a structured format.

For example, a finance dashboard may contain the following business selections:

{
  "CompanyCode": "1000",
  "FiscalYear": "2025",
  "Version": "Actual",
  "Currency": "USD",
  "Entity": "EU_Corp"
}

Rather than keeping these values scattered across input controls, filters, dropdowns, and application variables, ContextWallet consolidates them into a single centralized context object.

This context can then be accessed by any component within the application and can be passed directly to AI systems whenever required.

Core Design Principles

While designing ContextWallet, I focused on four fundamental principles.

1. Centralized Context Management

Modern analytical applications often contain dozens of user selections spread across multiple pages.

Without a centralized mechanism, each component must independently manage and retrieve these values.

ContextWallet provides a single source of truth for business context.

Instead of querying multiple controls throughout the application, developers can retrieve the entire context through a single interface.

2. Cross-Page Persistence

One of the challenges in multi-page SAC applications is maintaining context while navigating between pages.

A user may define a specific analytical scenario on one page and expect it to remain available throughout the application.

ContextWallet addresses this by persisting context within the browser session, allowing information to remain accessible across multiple dashboard pages.

This creates a more seamless user experience while reducing the need for repetitive filter selections.

3. Snapshot-Based State Management

Analytical workflows frequently involve recurring review scenarios.

Examples include:

  • Month-End Reviews

  • Quarterly Business Reviews

  • Forecast Reviews

  • Budget Planning Sessions

To support these use cases, ContextWallet introduces a snapshot mechanism.

Snapshots allow users or developers to capture the complete dashboard context at a specific point in time and restore it later.

This capability enables reproducible analytical scenarios and creates the foundation for future context comparison capabilities.

4. AI-Ready Context Generation

Perhaps the most important design goal was preparing SAC applications for AI integrations.

Traditional dashboards and AI systems often operate independently, even though they are working with the same business data.

ContextWallet helps bridge this gap by transforming dashboard selections into structured context payloads that can be consumed by AI assistants.

For example, when a user asks:

Why is revenue decreasing?

ContextWallet can automatically attach the active business context before sending the request to an AI system.

Instead of receiving only a question, the AI receives both the question and the analytical context in which the question was asked.

This significantly improves the relevance and accuracy of generated responses.

5. Future AI Security and Session Management

As organizations move toward AI-powered analytics, context management will become increasingly important not only for usability but also for security and governance.

Today, ContextWallet primarily focuses on capturing and managing dashboard context. However, its architecture opens opportunities for future enhancements related to AI session management and role-based AI interactions.

In enterprise environments, different users often require access to different AI capabilities depending on their responsibilities.

For example:

  • Finance Analysts may interact with financial planning models.

  • Controllers may access variance analysis assistants.

  • FP&A teams may work with forecasting and budgeting copilots.

  • Executives may receive high-level strategic insights.

  • Operations teams may interact with supply chain or operational AI models.

Rather than exposing a single AI model to every user, future implementations could leverage ContextWallet as a session-aware context layer capable of managing AI routing and authorization.

A session identifier or secure context token could be maintained alongside business context, allowing applications to determine:

  • Which AI model should receive a request.

  • Which data domains are accessible.

  • Which analytical capabilities are available.

  • Which business entities a user is authorized to view.

In this scenario, ContextWallet would not simply store dashboard filters but would become part of a broader AI governance framework.

A simplified future architecture could look like this:

User
  │
  ▼
SAP Analytics Cloud
  │
  ▼
ContextWallet
  │
  ├── Business Context
  ├── Session Context
  ├── Security Context
  └── User Role Context
  │
  ▼
AI Routing Layer
  │
  ├── Finance AI Assistant
  ├── FP&A Copilot
  ├── Executive Insights Model
  └── Operations Intelligence Agent

Such an approach would allow organizations to provide tailored AI experiences while maintaining security, compliance, and governance requirements.

Although these capabilities are currently part of the long-term vision rather than the current implementation, they demonstrate how Context Engineering can evolve beyond dashboard state management and become a foundational layer for enterprise AI ecosystems.

More Than a Storage Widget

While ContextWallet currently provides capabilities such as context storage, retrieval, snapshot management, and AI payload generation, its long-term vision extends far beyond traditional state management.

At its core, ContextWallet represents an exploration of Context Engineering within SAP Analytics Cloud—a concept that I believe will become increasingly important as organizations integrate AI into their analytics ecosystems.

Today, business context is often fragmented across filters, input controls, application variables, and user selections. Human users can naturally understand this context because it is visible on the dashboard. AI systems, however, typically receive only the final question, without access to the business environment in which that question was asked.

ContextWallet attempts to bridge this gap by establishing a centralized context layer that can be shared across dashboards, applications, users, and AI services.

The objective is not simply to remember values.

The objective is to create a context-aware analytical environment where dashboards, business processes, and AI systems operate using a common understanding of the user's current business scenario.

Looking ahead, the same architecture could evolve beyond dashboard context management and become part of a broader AI governance and orchestration framework.

Future implementations could leverage context, session identifiers, role information, and security metadata to route requests to specialized AI models based on business function and user authorization.

For example:

  • Finance teams could interact with financial planning copilots.

  • FP&A users could access forecasting and variance analysis assistants.

  • Executives could receive strategic insight generation models.

  • Operations teams could utilize supply-chain-specific AI agents.

In such an architecture, ContextWallet would not only manage business context but also contribute to AI session management, security enforcement, model routing, and role-based AI experiences.

In other words, the future vision is not merely a smarter dashboard.

It is the creation of a shared context layer that enables analytics applications and AI systems to collaborate more effectively, securely, and intelligently.

The next section explores the technical architecture behind ContextWallet and demonstrates how it can act as an intermediary layer between SAP Analytics Cloud and AI platforms such as Databricks Genie.

Architecture and Technical Design

 

ContextWallet Architecture

 

 

After establishing the concept of Context Engineering, the next step was designing a practical implementation that could operate within SAP Analytics Cloud while remaining flexible enough to support future AI integrations.

The primary design objective was to create a lightweight, reusable context layer that could sit between dashboard interactions and AI systems.

Rather than allowing filters, selections, and application state to remain scattered throughout the application, ContextWallet centralizes this information into a structured context object that can be accessed by both SAC components and external services.

High-Level Architecture

At a high level, ContextWallet acts as an intermediary layer between user interactions and AI systems.

The workflow begins when users interact with dashboard components such as:

  • Input Controls

  • Dropdowns

  • Filters

  • Tables

  • Application Variables

  • Planning Selections

These interactions generate business context that is relevant to the current analytical session.

Instead of allowing each component to manage its own state independently, ContextWallet collects and stores this information in a centralized context repository.

The stored context can then be consumed by:

  • SAC Application Scripts

  • Dashboard Components

  • Snapshot Management Functions

  • AI Payload Builders

  • External AI Platforms such as Databricks Genie

This creates a single source of truth for business context throughout the application.

Context Lifecycle

The architecture follows a simple but effective lifecycle:

Step 1: Context Capture

User selections are captured from dashboard components and stored in ContextWallet.

For example:

ContextWallet_1.setContext({
    CompanyCode: "1000",
    FiscalYear: "2025",
    Version: "Actual",
    Currency: "USD"
});

At this stage, ContextWallet becomes aware of the user's analytical environment.

Step 2: Context Persistence

Once stored, the context remains available throughout the application lifecycle.

This enables:

  • Cross-page navigation

  • Multi-step workflows

  • Dashboard state retention

  • Consistent AI interactions

Instead of repeatedly requesting filter information from multiple controls, applications can retrieve the required context directly from ContextWallet.

Step 3: Context Enrichment

As users continue interacting with the dashboard, additional information can be added to the context.

For example:

ContextWallet_1.mergeContext({
    Entity: "EU_Corp",
    AccountType: "Revenue"
});

The context evolves alongside the user's analytical journey.

Step 4: Context Consumption

When an AI interaction occurs, ContextWallet combines the user's question with the active business context.

For example:

var payload =
ContextWallet_1.getAIPayload(
    "Why is revenue decreasing?"
);

Result:

{
  "question": "Why is revenue decreasing?",
  "context": {
    "CompanyCode": "1000",
    "FiscalYear": "2025",
    "Currency": "USD",
    "Entity": "EU_Corp",
    "AccountType": "Revenue"
  }
}

Rather than receiving an isolated question, the AI receives both the question and the business scenario in which it was asked.

Snapshot Engine

One of the core architectural components of ContextWallet is the Snapshot Engine.

Snapshots allow complete analytical states to be preserved and restored.

For example, a finance team conducting a month-end review may save the current context as:

ContextWallet_1.saveSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

This snapshot captures the entire business context at that point in time.

Later, the same context can be restored using:

ContextWallet_1.loadSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

This capability provides reproducibility for analytical workflows and establishes the foundation for future context comparison and AI memory scenarios.

Preparing for AI Integration

The architecture was intentionally designed with AI integrations in mind.

Traditional AI implementations typically receive only the user's prompt.

ContextWallet introduces an additional layer:

Question
+
Business Context
+
Session Context
+
Application State

This combination allows future AI systems to generate responses that are aware of:

  • Current dashboard filters

  • Active reporting periods

  • Business entities

  • User workflow state

  • Saved analytical scenarios

In essence, ContextWallet transforms dashboard interactions into structured knowledge that can be consumed by AI systems.

Extending the Architecture

Although the current implementation focuses on context management, the architecture has been designed to support future enhancements such as:

  • Context history tracking

  • Session management

  • AI routing

  • Role-based AI experiences

  • Context-aware security controls

  • Multi-agent orchestration

These capabilities could enable organizations to move beyond isolated AI assistants and toward fully context-aware analytics ecosystems.

The next section demonstrates how this architecture can be applied to a practical use case by integrating SAP Analytics Cloud with Databricks Genie through a shared context layer.

SAC + Databricks Genie: A Practical Context Engineering Use Case

The concept of Context Engineering becomes most valuable when we examine how SAP Analytics Cloud can interact with AI-powered analytics platforms such as Databricks Genie.

Databricks Genie enables users to ask business questions in natural language and receive insights directly from enterprise data. This significantly lowers the barrier to analytics by allowing business users to interact with data conversationally instead of relying solely on dashboards and reports.

 

 

 

However, when integrating SAC with Genie, a challenge immediately emerges.

The Context Gap

Consider a financial analyst working inside a SAC dashboard.

The analyst has already configured the dashboard using several business filters:

  • Company Code = 1000

  • Fiscal Year = 2025

  • Version = Actual

  • Currency = USD

  • Entity = EU_Corp

  • Account Type = Revenue

After reviewing the dashboard, the analyst asks:

Why is revenue lower than last year?

While the question appears complete from the user's perspective, it lacks the surrounding business context that exists within the dashboard.

Without access to the dashboard state, Genie may not know:

  • Which company is being analyzed

  • Which fiscal year is active

  • Which currency is being used

  • Which reporting version is selected

  • Which business entity is in focus

As a result, the AI system may generate a generic response or require additional clarification from the user.

This creates friction in the analytical workflow because users are forced to repeatedly provide information that already exists within the dashboard.

Context-Aware AI Interactions

This is where ContextWallet introduces a different approach.

Instead of sending only the user's question to Genie, ContextWallet can package both the question and the active dashboard context into a structured payload.

For example:

{
  "question": "Why is revenue lower than last year?",
  "context": {
    "CompanyCode": "1000",
    "FiscalYear": "2025",
    "Version": "Actual",
    "Currency": "USD",
    "Entity": "EU_Corp",
    "AccountType": "Revenue"
  }
}

From an AI perspective, this additional information is extremely valuable.

The question is no longer interpreted in isolation.

It is interpreted within the business scenario that the user is currently analyzing.

This allows AI systems to generate more focused, relevant, and actionable responses.

Improving User Experience

One of the most significant advantages of this approach is the reduction of repetitive user interactions.

Without Context Engineering:

User:
Why is revenue decreasing?

AI:
Which company?

User:
Company Code 1000.

AI:
Which fiscal year?

User:
2025.

AI:
Which reporting version?

User:
Actual.

With Context Engineering:

User:
Why is revenue decreasing?

AI:
Analyzes Company Code 1000,
Fiscal Year 2025,
Version Actual,
and provides a contextualized answer.

The user interacts naturally while the application provides the necessary context behind the scenes.

Beyond Questions and Answers

The potential value of ContextWallet extends beyond conversational analytics.

Future integrations could enable AI systems to access:

  • Current dashboard state

  • Saved snapshots

  • Historical analytical scenarios

  • User workflow context

  • Session metadata

  • Role information

This would allow AI systems to understand not only the current question but also the broader analytical journey of the user.

For example:

What changed since the Month-End Review?

Instead of treating this as a generic question, the AI could compare the current dashboard context with a previously saved snapshot and generate insights based on the differences.

Similarly, planning teams could compare forecast versions, executive teams could analyze strategic review snapshots, and finance teams could revisit previous reporting scenarios.

From Dashboards to Context-Aware Analytics

Traditional dashboards excel at presenting information.

AI systems excel at interpreting information.

However, without a shared context layer, these systems often operate independently.

ContextWallet attempts to bridge this gap by establishing a common context model that can be understood by both SAP Analytics Cloud and external AI platforms.

In this architecture, the dashboard becomes the source of business context, ContextWallet becomes the context management layer, and Databricks Genie becomes the intelligence layer.

Together, they create a foundation for a new generation of context-aware analytics applications where users can move seamlessly between dashboards, business workflows, and conversational AI experiences.

Future Vision: From Context Management to AI Orchestration

 

 

 

 

The current implementation of ContextWallet focuses on a relatively simple objective: capturing, preserving, and sharing business context within SAP Analytics Cloud.

However, while building and experimenting with the framework, it became apparent that the underlying concept could extend far beyond dashboard state management.

As organizations continue integrating AI into enterprise analytics, a new challenge emerges:

How do we manage, govern, and orchestrate multiple AI systems while maintaining security, context, and business relevance?

This is where the future vision of ContextWallet begins.

Beyond Dashboard Context

Today, ContextWallet primarily stores:

  • Dashboard filters

  • User selections

  • Application state

  • Analytical snapshots

  • AI payload context

In the future, the same architecture could be extended to manage additional layers of information such as:

  • Session Context

  • User Context

  • Security Context

  • Role Context

  • AI Routing Metadata

This would allow ContextWallet to evolve from a context repository into a centralized context orchestration layer.

AI Session Management

One of the most interesting future applications involves AI session management.

Enterprise AI interactions are rarely isolated events.

Users often engage in a sequence of related analytical conversations, building upon previous questions and insights.

For example:

Question 1:
Why is revenue decreasing?

Question 2:
Which products contributed most?

Question 3:
How does this compare with last quarter?

These questions are connected.

Without session awareness, each interaction must be treated independently.

With session-aware context management, AI systems can maintain continuity across analytical conversations and provide more relevant responses.

Future versions of ContextWallet could store session identifiers and maintain conversational context throughout the user's analytical journey.

Role-Based AI Experiences

Not every user requires access to the same AI capabilities.

A finance analyst, a controller, a planner, and an executive often have very different responsibilities and information requirements.

For example:

Finance Analyst

  • Variance Analysis

  • Financial Reporting

  • Cost Analysis

FP&A Team

  • Forecasting

  • Budget Planning

  • Scenario Simulation

Executive Leadership

  • Strategic Insights

  • KPI Summaries

  • Business Performance Reviews

Rather than exposing a single AI assistant to every user, future implementations could dynamically route requests to specialized AI systems based on role and authorization.

This would enable organizations to create tailored AI experiences while maintaining governance and security controls.

AI Routing and Model Selection

As AI ecosystems mature, organizations are unlikely to rely on a single model.

Instead, different AI systems may be optimized for different tasks.

For example:

Financial Analysis Model
        │
Forecasting Model
        │
Planning Copilot
        │
Executive Insights Assistant
        │
Operational Intelligence Agent

Future versions of ContextWallet could act as an intelligent routing layer.

Based on context, user role, and session metadata, requests could automatically be directed to the most appropriate AI system.

This would allow users to interact with a unified interface while the platform manages the complexity behind the scenes.

Security and Governance

Enterprise AI adoption introduces important governance requirements.

Organizations must ensure that users only access information relevant to their responsibilities.

ContextWallet could potentially support this through:

  • Role-aware context management

  • Secure session identifiers

  • Access-control metadata

  • AI authorization rules

For example, an executive assistant might receive organization-wide insights, while a finance analyst receives only information related to their assigned entities.

This approach would help ensure that AI interactions remain aligned with existing security and compliance frameworks.

Towards a Context-Aware Analytics Ecosystem

The long-term vision is not simply a smarter dashboard.

The vision is a context-aware analytics ecosystem where dashboards, applications, business processes, and AI systems operate using a shared understanding of the business environment.

In such an ecosystem:

  • Dashboards generate context.

  • ContextWallet manages context.

  • AI systems consume context.

  • Business users receive contextualized insights.

Instead of repeatedly explaining the business scenario to every application and AI assistant, context becomes a reusable enterprise asset.

Getting Started with ContextWallet

For readers interested in experimenting with ContextWallet, the project is available as an open-source SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget.

GitHub Repository

Source Code:

https://github.com/sampurno417/ContextWallet

The repository contains:

  • ContextWallet Widget Source Code

  • Widget Manifest (JSON)

  • Production Bundle

  • Documentation

  • Example Usage Patterns

Installation

Step 1: Download the Widget Manifest

Open the repository and download the latest ContextWallet JSON manifest.

Step 2: Import the Widget into SAP Analytics Cloud

Navigate to:

Analytics Designer
→ Custom Widgets
→ Add Custom Widget
→ Upload JSON Manifest

Once imported, ContextWallet becomes available within your SAC application.

Step 3: Add ContextWallet to the Application

Drag and drop the widget onto any page.

The widget itself does not need to be visible to end users and can be hidden after being added to the application.

ContextWallet is primarily designed as a backend utility component accessed through Analytics Designer scripting.

Basic Usage

Store Context

ContextWallet_1.setContext({
    CompanyCode: "1000",
    FiscalYear: "2025",
    Currency: "USD"
});

Retrieve Context

var context =
ContextWallet_1.getContext();

console.log(context);

Add Additional Fields

ContextWallet_1.addContextField(
    "Entity",
    "EU_Corp"
);

Merge Context

ContextWallet_1.mergeContext({
    AccountType: "Revenue"
});

Working with Snapshots

Save the current dashboard state:

ContextWallet_1.saveSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

Retrieve available snapshots:

var snapshots =
ContextWallet_1.getSnapshots();

Restore a snapshot:

ContextWallet_1.loadSnapshot(
    "MonthEndReview"
);

Preparing AI Payloads

ContextWallet can generate structured payloads suitable for AI integrations.

var payload =
ContextWallet_1.getAIPayload(
    "Why is revenue decreasing?"
);

console.log(payload);

Example output:

{
  "question": "Why is revenue decreasing?",
  "context": {
    "CompanyCode": "1000",
    "FiscalYear": "2025",
    "Currency": "USD",
    "Entity": "EU_Corp"
  }
}

This payload can be passed to external AI systems such as Databricks Genie, custom LLM applications, or enterprise copilots.

Current Status

ContextWallet is currently an experimental framework focused on exploring Context Engineering concepts within SAP Analytics Cloud.

The project continues to evolve, with future enhancements planned around:

  • AI Session Management

  • Role-Based AI Routing

  • Security Context

  • Context History

  • Multi-Agent AI Integrations

  • Databricks Genie Connectivity

Feedback, suggestions, and collaboration ideas are always welcome.

Conclusion

As Generative AI becomes increasingly embedded within enterprise analytics platforms, context will play a critical role in determining the quality, relevance, and trustworthiness of AI-generated insights.

ContextWallet began as an experiment in solving a practical problem within SAP Analytics Cloud: preserving and sharing dashboard context.

Along the way, it revealed a broader architectural concept that I refer to as Context Engineering.

While the current implementation focuses on context management, snapshots, and AI payload generation, the underlying idea has the potential to evolve into a foundational layer for future AI-enabled analytics applications.

The journey is still at an early stage, but one thing is becoming increasingly clear:

In the era of enterprise AI, understanding the question is important.

Understanding the context behind the question is essential.

This project was developed as part of my exploration into SAP Analytics Cloud, AI-assisted analytics, and Databricks Genie integrations. I hope it encourages further discussion around Context Engineering and the role of context-aware architectures in the future of enterprise analytics.

entry.title: Re: What's different in SAP Sales and Distribution (SD) in S/4 HANA
entry.content:
From an implementation perspective, I feel the biggest learning isn't adapting to the new SD functionality but understanding how S/4HANA changes the overall implementation approach. Business Partner, Fiori, embedded analytics, and cross-module integration require functional consultants to work much more closely with technical, security, and data migration teams than in traditional ECC implementations.

One challenge we experienced during S/4HANA implementation wasn't SD configuration itself but master data readiness. Business Partner synchronization, customer master conversion, and incomplete sales area data caused more delays during testing than the actual SD process changes. Investing time in data cleansing early can significantly reduce project issues.

entry.title: Re: How can I query a customers balance with an API? App-...
entry.content:
Hi Holger,

Short answer: There is no released 1:1 API for the app Display Customer Balances (F0703), and FARVD_CUST_BAL_0 is an internal, unreleased view — but you can get customer balances via the released Trial Balance – Read API, which exposes Customer as a dimension.

Is there a corresponding API for F0703?

No. The OData service behind F0703 (FAR_CUST_BALANCE_SRV) is UI-only and not released for external consumption, and the underlying view is not C1-released, so it can't be used in Custom CDS Views either — your finding is correct. It is also not listed in the released API package on the SAP Business Accelerator Hub. Reference: S/4HANA Cloud OData API package

How can I query a customer's balance with an API?

With the Trial Balance – Read API (recommended). The OData V2 service C_TRIALBALANCE_CDS (API "Trial Balance – Read") returns starting balance, debits, credits and ending balance aggregated by whatever you put in $select — and the result set includes Customer, Customer (Description) and Customer Group as fields. So $select=CompanyCode,Customer,EndingBalanceInCoCodeCrcy with filters on Ledger, CompanyCode and the posting-date parameters gives you exactly a customer balance list. Reference: Trial Balance – Read (SAP Help), and API overview on Business Accelerator Hub

Alternative – line items: the API Journal Entry Item – Read (A2X) (API_JOURNALENTRYITEMBASIC_SRV) lets you read journal entry items filtered by Customer; you aggregate on the consumer side. Useful if you also need open/cleared item detail, not just the balance.

Alternative – Custom CDS View: instead of FARVD_CUST_BAL_0, build a Custom CDS View on a released view such as I_OperationalAcctgDocItem (check release status in the View Browser app) and expose it as an OData API. Reference: Create CDS views in S/4HANA Cloud and consume them as APIs, Custom CDS Views (SAP Help)

Constraints to verify:

  • Trial Balance API needs a communication arrangement; the scenario discussed for this API is SAP_COM_0303 — verify in your Communication Arrangements app. Reference: Trial Balance Read API issues (SAP Community)
  • Balances are computed from posting-date ranges (period balances), not stored per period as in F0703's fiscal-year view — reconcile your date ranges with fiscal periods.
  • If your real use case is a customer-facing portal (open items, invoices, payments): SAP released a set of AR APIs for invoice-to-cash portals in Public Cloud as of 2508.02. Reference: SAP S/4HANA Cloud for Customer Payments – Transition and Future with APIs

See also SAP's blog series Balance Reporting – Part 2: standard API and Part 3: custom reports.

Best regards,

Michal

entry.title: The SAP Error That Taught Me More Than Any Training Course
entry.content:
A few weeks ago, I was convinced I had configured everything correctly.

The purchase requisition was created.
The purchase order looked perfect.
The pricing was in place.
Everything seemed ready for testing.

Then came a single error message.

At first, I thought it was another routine configuration issue. I checked the obvious settings, verified the master data, and compared it with a working scenario. Nothing looked wrong.

Hours passed.

Instead of searching for a quick fix, I decided to trace the entire business process—from master data to configuration, document flow, and integration points.

That was when I realized something important.

SAP rarely fails because of one transaction.

Most issues are a chain reaction.

A missing sales area extension for a material can stop order creation.

A posting period that's closed can prevent financial documents from being generated.

An incorrect Business Partner role can impact sales, purchasing, and finance simultaneously.

The error message is often only the final symptom. The actual cause usually exists much earlier in the process.

This changed the way I approach SAP implementations.

Today, whenever I encounter an issue, I don't immediately search for the transaction code that fixes it. Instead, I ask a different question:

"Which business process led to this error?"

That small shift in thinking has helped me solve problems faster and understand SAP more deeply.

As someone who is continuously learning SAP SD, MM, and their integration with other modules, I've realized that technical knowledge alone isn't enough. Understanding how business processes connect across the system is what truly makes a consultant effective.

Every error message has a story behind it.

The sooner we learn to read that story, the better consultants we become.

What was the SAP error that taught you the biggest lesson during your implementation or support experience? I'd love to hear your story.

entry.title: Configuration SSO between SAC to S/4HANA
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

I am planning to establish Single Sign-On (SSO) between SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and SAP S/4HANA, as well as between SAP SuccessFactors and SAP S/4HANA (where SAP S/4HANA is deployed on a Private Cloud). Azure AD work as identity provider and SAP IAS work as proxy

I would appreciate your guidance on the overall setup, as I have a few questions and would like to clarify some points:

  1. We currently have multiple SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS) and Identity Provisioning Service (IPS) tenants provisioned by SAP. For example:

    • Landscape A (Test): IAS (Ariba Applications) and IPS (Ariba Applications, BTP, BIZX)
    • Landscape B (Production): Similar bundles
    • Landscape C (Test): IAS (BTP, BIZX) and IPS (BTP, BIZX) and 2 more 

    My question is: If I create a new SAP BTP subaccount and subscribe to IAS, will a new IAS tenant be created automatically and any additional license count ? If I want to use one of the existing IAS tenants instead of creating a new one, what is the recommended approach during the subscription and configuration process?

  2. For establishing SSO between SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and SAP S/4HANA, is it necessary to create a separate SAP BTP subaccount and subscribe to SAC within that subaccount, or can the existing BTP landscape be used?

I would appreciate any guidance or best practices regarding this architecture and configuration.

Thank you in advance for your support.

Regards Shahid

entry.title: Re: Mass Asset Change Bot and mass asset transfer between...
entry.content:
Hi Ioanna,

Short answer: Yes — the Mass Update Asset Master Data (57O) bot is SAP-delivered RPA content that is being retired; there is no successor bot, and SAP's direction is the released public Fixed Asset APIs; for a bulk asset-class change there is no bot and no one-step "change class", so it has to be modeled as an asset transfer.

When will the "Mass Update Asset Master Data (57O)" bot be removed, and is there an alternative bot?

No replacement bot.
The 57O package is part of the SAP-delivered bot library that ran on SAP Intelligent RPA, which SAP has sunset; that content line moved to SAP Build Process Automation and SAP now steers S/4HANA mass operations to released public APIs instead of delivered bots. So treat 57O as deprecated and do not build a new go-live design on it.
Reference: Community portal and Reference (package): https://api.sap.com/package/f5cd5ab09d2649a1aeef224d67411d72/overview

Please note:

  • There is no removal date declared yet. The binding date is the deprecation/removal date shown on the 57O package inside your SAP Build Process Automation Store (and the SAP Note linked from that banner) — read it there for your tenant.
  • Your go-live is 01.01.2027: design on the API now, not on the bot.

Replacement for mass asset master data change:

There is, with the API.
Use the released Fixed Asset Master Data API (OData v4, action-based CreateFixedAsset / ChangeFixedAsset; SOAP variant also exists) for bulk create/change of asset master data.
Reference (how-to): KBA 3625447 and List of Asset APIs

How should we proceed with mass asset transfer from one asset class to another?

Not with bot and no one-step class change. In Public Cloud you cannot change the asset class of an existing asset — account determination is fixed at creation. Reference (FAQ): Community portal

Standard path: model it as an asset transfer — create the target asset in the new class and transfer the values from the source asset (source is deactivated). Reference: SAP Help. To ease your bulk needs, create the target-class assets in bulk via the Fixed Asset Master Data API, then post the transfers with the Post Transfer app asset by asset. Asset transfer can be reach through the Fixed Asset master data

Alternative: You retire the old asset in the original class using the Fixed Asset – Post Asset Retirement API, and create and capitalize a new one in the target class via the Fixed Asset – Post Asset Acquisition API, which allows the process to be mass-automated. However, this is not a true asset transfer—it generates two separate documents without a linked transfer document and uses different accounting logic (APC and accumulated depreciation are not transferred 1:1), so only implement this after the accounting methodology has been approved.

Summary
Treat 57O as deprecated and build the go-live on the released Fixed Asset Master Data API for mass create/change; For the asset-class transfer, there is no bot and no one-step change — create the target-class assets via the API and post them as Asset transfer manually.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Bring your own Data Provider to SAP Master Data Governance for Business Partner
entry.content:
Your business partner data is only as good as the sources behind it. And here is the great news: SAP Master Data Governance now lets you connect any external reference data provider you trust - whether that's a commercial registry, a regional authority database, or a specialized industry source.

No core modifications. No custom SAP MDG code. All provider specific integration code is outsourced to SAP Cloud Integration.

How It Works

SAP Master Data Governance provides a provider-agnostic interface. In SAP Cloud Integration you copy and adapt a ready-made integration template for your provider - request format, authentication, response mapping - and deploy it. Your users search for business partners, pick the best match, and key data like tax numbers and identification numbers flow directly into the creation process.

Why It's Worth It

  • Use the provider that fits your business - not just the one that comes pre-configured by SAP
  • Run multiple providers in parallel - users choose at runtime, no IT involvement needed
  • Stay upgrade-safe - your customizations are isolated from SAP's core framework
  • Get up and running fast - copy SAP's delivered template and adapt only what's provider-specific

Interested? Explore the How-To-Guide  to get up to speed with your integration.

For questions about SAP Master Data Governance and data provider integration, please use our our  discussion forum in the SAP Community, and feel free to give us your feedback by commenting on this blog post.

To stay up to date with what’s going on in master data management and governance, simply follow the SAP Community Topic Page for SAP Master Data Governance.

entry.title: Re: PAYMENT METHOD SEPA CREDIT TRANSFER
entry.content:
Good day @luigi-caligiuri 

Thank you for your question.

The behaviour you described is not supported for the same SEPA relevant payment method.

In simple terms, if the payment method used in the service contract or sales document is treated as SEPA relevant, the system expects a valid SEPA mandate.  Note that a service transaction must be SEPA relevant before a SEPA mandate can be assigned, and this relevance is determined from the payment method used in the service document. For service contract item processing, if the item is SEPA relevant, a SEPA mandate must be provided in the SEPA mandate field or property.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/adbae5bcd5994f159bf2847a11397b61/10d7cd50a4644e1b822d58c6c0063c68.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/6d57d4ab71e5408581e2018320a0ce49/48ea33fbf29646f192770fb8efedc7a7.html?locale=en-US

So, if payment method S is configured in your tenant as a SEPA direct debit relevant payment method, there is no standard app setting in Manage Service Contracts to keep payment method S while suppressing the mandate requirement.

Here is what I suggested:

  1. Check the payment method setup first. In your configuration environment, check payment method S for Italy Set Up Payment Methods for Each Country/Region for Payment Transactions. Confirm whether it is really intended for incoming SEPA direct debit and whether the SEPA mandate requirement is active. This configuration activity for country specific payment method setup. See the following reference for more details https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4603118feda9495892abadbe8f8c3d14/e3d9e3ae49af45bc8df33499aab13874.html?locale=en-US 
  2. If mandate is not available at contract creation. Use a non-SEPA direct debit payment method during contract or sales order creation, then change to the SEPA relevant payment method and assign the mandate once the mandate exists. Note that you can assign SEPA mandates in service contracts and are forwarded to the follow-on billing document. See the following reference for more details https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/adbae5bcd5994f159bf2847a11397b61/bfd98ae6ed9f4eb6b92436c7815dbf66.html?locale=en-US
  3. You can create or maintain the mandate before SEPA collection. Make use of Manage SEPA Mandates to create, copy, display, or change SEPA mandates for the customer. The payment run evaluates SEPA payment methods when incoming and outgoing payments are created.                                                        
  4. Retest the service contract, open Manage Service Contracts, create or change the contract, enter the payment method, and assign the valid SEPA mandate.                                                            

     

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Configuration SSO between SAC to S/4HANA
entry.content:
This is an interesting one because SAP itself is all over the place.

Let's just say I found myself in a similar situation with about 15 IAS-systems and various BTP, SF, Ariba and other Cloud systems, spread over different customer numbers.

It took a while, but we're basically down to one IAS (whether you chose one or two, and split Prod is dealer's choice) and 3 IAS for each SF one.
SAP maintained for very long that having a seperate IAS for SF was highly recommended, but is slowly walking that back (and probably will help with migration at some point). If you can, pick the Productive IAS as your main system - I would if I could start again. You have the benefit of having all users and less 'traffic' between systems. It will also help with Joule over systems.

https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/btp-admin-guide/onboard-to-sap-cloud-identity-services?locale=en-US

https://me.sap.com/notes/2954815/E#&/ecsworkspacecomponent/?variant=standard

entry.title: Re: Goods issue date calculation after PO creation
entry.content:
How GI Date is Calculated

In SAP, the Delivery Date on the PO is the date the vendor is expected to deliver. The GI Date is derived by subtracting planned delivery time, GR processing time, and sometimes transportation lead time depending on configuration. If you advance the delivery date by one day, the system recalculates backwards, so the GI date may shift by more than one day if multiple lead times are involved.

You mentioned the expectation of Delivery Date = GI Date + 2. That means you want a fixed offset of 2 days between GI and Delivery.

How to Achieve this

Set GR Processing Time = 2 days in the material master (MRP2 view).

Ensure Planned Delivery Time is maintained correctly (usually vendor lead time).

With backward scheduling, SAP will then calculate GI Date = Delivery Date – GR Processing Time. So if GR Processing Time = 2, you will always get Delivery Date = GI Date + 2.

entry.title: work zone Standard Edition: Local copy of tile assigned to role and Group/Catalog in not visible
entry.content:
Hi There,

We in a UI5 migration project from NEO to Cloud Foundry.

Scenario:
In neo we have an app configured as 2 tiles with different visualization, because of user Group.
Now in CF and Work zone standard edition, we tried below and none worked.

  1. Defined 2 inbound intents in manifest, but in work zone displays single app/tile with 1st intent only, we raised a ticket with SAP and they said this is not supported.
  2. We deployed app with 1st visualization, created a local copy. then redeployed with 2nd visualization.
  • In work zone I could see both the tiles now as expected.
  • I could assign the tiles to roles, catalogs and groups, no issues as such.
  • But in Launch pad I couldn't see the local copy version with 1st visualization.

Have someone tried such scenario? I couldn't find any solution as such yet.

Other than marinating 2 apps, dual maintenance and technical debt.
Please advise.

Thank you,

Sai

entry.title: Unit Conversion in SAP Datasphere using T006 (LB to KG Example)
entry.content:
Unit Conversion in SAP Datasphere Using T006/T006D – LB to KG Example

Introduction:

During a recent SAP Datasphere reporting requirement, I needed to present quantities in a consistent unit of measure even though the source data was stored in different units. Rather than implementing custom conversion logic, I explored how SAP standard unit conversion tables could be leveraged through the CONVERT_UNIT function.

 

In SAP Datasphere, data frequently originates from multiple source systems that use different units of measure. To ensure accurate analytics and reporting, these quantities often need to be converted into a common business unit. In this example, the requirement is to convert quantities from LB to KG using SAP standard unit conversion logic.

 

This blog explains the SAP standard tables involved in unit conversion, the implementation approach in SAP Datasphere, the validation process, and the key lessons learned during the exercise.

 

Business Use Case:

Consider a manufacturing company that receives material quantities from suppliers in LB, while corporate reporting standards require all quantities to be reported in KG.

Without a standard conversion mechanism, reports from different locations become difficult to compare. SAP Datasphere's unit conversion capability helps standardize these quantities using SAP-maintained conversion factors and dimensions.

For example:

  • Source system stores quantity as 568 LB
  • Business users want reporting output in KG
  • Manual conversion in every report can lead to inconsistency
  • A centralized conversion logic in SAP Datasphere gives consistent and reusable results

This type of conversion is commonly needed in:

  • Inventory reporting
  • Procurement analytics
  • Material movement reporting
  • Cross-country operational reporting
  • Consolidated quantity reporting across multiple source systems

 

Prerequisites to understand:

Before implementing unit conversion in SAP Datasphere, it is important to understand the SAP standard tables used for unit conversion.

The two primary SAP standard tables involved in unit conversion are:

  • T006D – Dimension table
  • T006 – Unit of measurement table

Because these tables are maintained centrally within SAP, they provide a consistent and trusted source of conversion factors across applications and reporting scenarios.

 

T006D (Dimensions):

This table stores unit dimensions. DIMID is a primary key in this table, and it represents the ‘Dimension’, and MSSIE stores one unique SI/base unit for that dimension.

For example, for the dimension MASS, the SI/base unit is KG.

So, when we convert from LB to KG, both units belong to the same dimension: MASS.

This is important because unit conversion should happen only between units that belong to the same logical dimension.

 

Once the dimension and base unit have been identified in T006D, the next step is to understand how individual units within that dimension are maintained in T006.

 

T006 (Units of Measurement):

T006 stores the units of measurement and their conversion factors.

For the MASS dimension, units such as G, KG, OZ, LB, and TON can be maintained.

 

How to Implement Unit Conversion in Datasphere:

  1. Create a simple Fact View on the source table, for example ACDOCA, and enable the required fields.  In my current implementation, the source quantity field is MSL and the source unit field is RUNIT.
  2. Create 2 calculated columns in the ‘Formula’ node
  • MSL_Converted – to convert quantity
  • RUNIT_Converted – to display the converted target unit

 

 

Use the CONVERT_UNIT function to convert the source quantity from LB to KG.

In this example, the target unit is hardcoded as KG for simplicity.

However, this logic is suitable only for a simple demo scenario. In real projects, avoid hardcoding too many unit mappings in calculated columns. Instead, it is better to use an Input Parameter for the target unit, if business users need flexibility to convert quantities into different units such as KG, G, or TON

 

 

To show the converted unit in the output, create another calculated column.

 

This makes the output easier for business users to understand because the converted quantity and converted unit are shown together.

 

After deploying the view, perform a data preview and validate the converted result.

Manual Data Validation:

 

 

For the LB unit, the important fields used in this example are:

Field

Meaning

Value in this example

MSEHI

Unit of measure

LB

DIMID

Dimension

MASS

ZAEHL

Numerator

453592

NENNR

Denominator

1000000

EXP10

Power of 10 scaling factor

0

 

Here Quantity in Source Unit (ACDOCA-MSL) = 568

                                                           Source Unit = LB

                                                            Target Unit = KG

From T006:

ZAEHL = 453592

NENNR = 1000000

EXP10 = 0

 

 

The LB to KG conversion factor is derived as:

 

Quantity in Target Unit = Quantity in Source Unit * (ZAEHL / NENNR)

Quantity in Target Unit   = 568*(453592/1000000)

Quantity in Target Unit = 568 *(0.453592)

Quantity in Target Unit = 257.640256 KG

This matches the value returned by the CONVERT_UNIT function, so the conversion is verified and correct.

Note: SAP also stores an additional field called EXP10 (exponent), which is used for scaling in some unit conversions.
In this example, EXP10 = 0, so it does not impact the calculation.

 

Area

What to Check

Why It Matters

Source unit

Ensure the source unit value exists in T006

If the source unit is missing or invalid, conversion can fail

Dimension

Ensure source and target units belong to the same dimension

LB and KG belong to MASS, so conversion is valid

Conversion factor

Validate ZAEHL, NENNR, and EXP10

These fields determine the actual conversion factor

Target unit

Avoid unnecessary hardcoding

Hardcoding is okay for demos, but parameters are better for reusable models

Error handling

Use appropriate error handling based on requirement

fail on error helps identify missing or incorrect unit conversion data early

Validation

Always compare Datasphere output with manual calculation

This builds confidence before using the logic in reports

 

When Is Unit Conversion Needed?

Unit conversion is useful when the same business measure is captured in different units across systems or processes.

Some common scenarios are:

  • Source data stores quantity in vendor-specific units
  • Reporting requires a standard unit across countries or plants
  • Different SAP or non-SAP systems send different units
  • Planning and analytics require normalized quantities
  • Business users compare material quantities across regions

For example, if one plant stores inventory in LB and another plant reports in KG, the analytical output becomes inconsistent unless the quantities are converted into a common unit.

 

Common Pitfall:

One common issue is attempting to convert between units that belong to different dimensions. For example, SAP will not perform a valid conversion between MASS and LENGTH dimensions because their measurement contexts are unrelated. Therefore, validating the DIMID relationship in T006D should be one of the first troubleshooting steps when conversion results are unexpected.

Another common issue is hardcoding conversion factors instead of using SAP standard conversion logic. While manual calculations may work for simple examples, they can become difficult to maintain when conversion definitions change or additional units need to be supported.

 

Lessons Learned:

During this implementation, I observed the following:

  • Always verify that source and target units belong to the same dimension in T006D.
  • Validate Datasphere results using T006 conversion factors before deploying to production.
  • INPUT PARAMETERS provide more flexibility than hardcoded target units.
  • EXP10 should be reviewed for conversions involving scaling factors.
  • CONVERT_UNIT is preferable to manual calculations because it leverages SAP standard conversion logic.

 

Conclusion:

In this exercise, I used SAP standard tables T006 and T006D together with the CONVERT_UNIT function to convert quantities from LB to KG in SAP Datasphere.

 

The converted value was validated against SAP-maintained conversion factors, confirming the accuracy of the result. By relying on SAP's standard conversion framework instead of custom formulas, organizations can build more scalable, reusable, and reliable reporting solutions when working with multiple units of measure.

 

I hope this example helps you understand not only how unit conversion works in SAP Datasphere, but also how SAP standard tables can be used to validate and troubleshoot conversion results effectively.

entry.title: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model: What's New as of 2026-07-06?
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

Welcome to this month's What's New blog post for SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM). We're delighted to present the latest improvements and innovations that continue to enhance the platform, helping you work more efficiently while delivering a smoother and more intuitive experience.

Let's explore what's new! 🚀

Process Scheduler | Environment Lifecycle Management

Previously, recurring Process Scheduler jobs were not part of the environment lifecycle. Every environment copy or import meant recreating all job definitions manually in the target environment, and deleting an environment left orphaned jobs behind in the Process Scheduler.

With this update, recurring Process Scheduler jobs are now fully integrated across four environment operations: Copy, Export, Import, and Deletion. Job definitions travel with their environment and are automatically removed when the environment is deleted. Copy and import operations include an opt-in checkbox to include recurring jobs, and all operations are tracked in the Application Monitor.

COPY:

IMPORT:

For a full walkthrough of each operation, see our dedicated blog post. For more information, refer to the Process Scheduler documentation on the SAP Help Portal.

Content Management and Application Monitor | Improved Logs for Environment Import

We've improved the logging for environment imports in Content Management. The import log entry now includes the original imported file name, giving you better visibility and traceability during the import process.

What changed?

Previously, when an environment import was initiated, the Application Monitor log showed:

"Model Import started for Environment=DSE, Version=0010"

With this improvement, the log now displays: 

"Model Import started for Environment=DSE, Version=0010 based on the original content DSE-0001-20260527T132918847Z.zip" 



Why does this matter? 

When managing multiple imports — especially in automated TMS (Transport Management System) deployments — it was difficult to trace back which specific file initiated a particular import. The updated log entry now makes it easy to:

  1. Identify the exact source file of any import.
  2. Audit and troubleshoot import activities more efficiently.
  3. Correlate Application Monitor entries with the files used in your deployment pipeline.

Removal of the Optional Feature for the Python Executor

The Optional Feature for the Python Executor has been removed from the Settings screen. The Python 3.13 implementation is now the permanent and only supported executor for the Remote Function Adapter (RFA) Python function type in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model (SAP PaPM Cloud SM).

Previously, administrators could activate  the new Python executor and switch back to the previous implementation if required. As the migration to Python 3.13 has been completed, this configuration option is no longer available, and all RFA Python functions now run using the latest implementation by default.

What Changed?

  • The use New Remote Function Adapter Python Implementation toggle has been removed from Administration > Settings > Optional Features.
  • Python 3.13 is now the permanent and only supported Python executor.
  • Administrators no longer need to configure the Python executor version.

Benefits:

  • Simplifies system administration by removing an obsolete configuration option.
  • Ensures a consistent Python execution environment across all tenants.
  • Eliminates the possibility of reverting to the legacy Python executor, providing a single supported runtime for all RFA Python executions.

Modeling | Partitioning Function: Dynamic Packaging Type

The Partitioning function now supports a new Packaging Type dropdown, added directly after the Run Mode field in the properties panel. This lets you choose whether partition ranges are fixed at design time or calculated at runtime from the actual Input function data.

  • Static (default): Ranges are defined at design time as inner nodes in the Partitioning function. No change to existing models are required.
  • Dynamic: Ranges are calculated at runtime from the Input function entries. Any existing Range nodes are removed when a Static Partitioning is updated to Dynamic.

When Dynamic is selected, Run Mode values PPBP, PPDP, SPXP, and SUXP are not available, and Range Levels and Destinations are not supported.



The Field property has been renamed to Packaging Field and repositioned directly after Packaging Type. Multiple Partitioning functions can reference the same Packaging Field as long as each uses a different Run Mode or Packaging Type.


For more details about the Dynamic Packaging Partitioning, you may check SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model - Introducing Dynamic Packaging Type in Partitioning.

Joule | AI Enhancements

Joule now supports uploading .txt and .docx files as additional context for your conversations, expanding the range of file formats available for upload.


Report Management | Create, Rewrite, or Delete Report Sections

You can now use Joule in Act mode to create new text sections, rewrite existing ones, or delete sections from your report directly through the Joule conversation panel — without manually editing the report.

Previously, report text could only be modified by entering Edit mode manually. The Inline Report Editing feature was introduced, allowing you to rewrite or improve text directly within your report.

With this update, you can instruct Joule conversationally to shape your report content for a single section, or multiple sections in a single prompt.

To get started, open the Joule panel in the Report Management application, choose Act to switch to Act mode, and type your instruction referring to the target section by its title.

The following actions are supported:

Action

Example Prompt

Rewrite an existing section    

"Please rewrite the Processes and Activities section giving it more of the business touch."

Add a new text section

"Add a new Key Risks and Mitigations section after the Summary section."

Delete a text section

"Delete the Introduction section."

Joule applies non-destructive actions (create, rewrite) immediately and automatically refreshes the report screen — the requested modification is applied and displayed in the report without any manual refresh needed.

For destructive actions such as deletion, Joule asks for your confirmation before proceeding — choose Approve to apply or Reject to cancel.



The following notes apply:

  • If you add a new section without specifying a position, Joule adds it at the end of the report.
  • Deleted sections cannot be recovered.
  • For rewritten sections, you can ask Joule to restore the previous content based on the conversation context.
  • If you are in Edit mode with unsaved changes, save or discard them first — Joule will prompt you if needed.

For more information, refer to the relevant pages on our SAP Help Portal: Create, Rewrite, or Delete Report Sections | Prompting Recommendations | Inline Report Editing

Modeling Screen | Remote Function Adapter: General Function

A new function type is now available in the Remote Function Adapter (RFA) palette in SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model: the General Function.


The General Function connects SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model to a custom Function Module on your On-Premise SAP system through an RFC destination configured in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). This allows existing On-Premise ABAP logic — written as a BAPI wrapper — to be called directly from your calculation model, with input data passed in through IT_DATA and results returned through ET_DATA into the result table. No rebuilding of On-Premise logic in the Cloud is required.

To use the General Function, the following must be in place:

  1. A custom Function Module on your On-Premise system, configured as Remote-Enabled (SE37) and structured with the required IT_DATA, ET_DATA, and ET_MESSAGE parameters.
  2. An RFC Destination in SAP BTP Destination Service, with the On-Premise system mapped via SAP Cloud Connector.



Once configured, the General Function can be run through all standard SAP Profitability and Performance Management Cloud Standard Model run entry points, including direct Run, Run with Parameters and Selections, Process Template, Process Instance, and Process Scheduler.

For a full walkthrough of configuration, BAPI compatibility requirements, and end-to-end verification, see our dedicated blog post: SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model – Introducing the Remote Function Adapter: General Function.

File Uploads | Enhanced File Validation Across Applications

File upload and import operations across SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model now include enhanced security validations, providing stronger protection against malformed, misnamed, or potentially malicious files.

Previously, uploads were protected by malware scanning only. With this update, additional validation checks are applied automatically during every file upload or import operation including real file type detection (verifying the actual file content, not just the extension), file name validation, and improved handling of archive files.

The following applications are affected:

Application

Use Case

Supported Formats

Data Management

Upload data to Model Tables and Queries

CSV, XLSX

Content Management

Upload / import environments

ZIP

Process Management

Mass upload of process instance settings

XLSX

Report Management

Upload images to Report panels

BMP, JPEG, JPG, GIF, PNG, TIFF, WEBP

Joule

Attach files to Joule conversations

CSV, XLSX, PDF

No action is required on your side as the validations run automatically. Each step of the file upload process is logged, and the system displays an appropriate message to inform you of the outcome whether the upload was successful, or the file was rejected due to a validation or security scanning failure.

For more information, refer to our SAP Help Portal: Secure File Handling  

That's all for this update!

We hope these enhancements help further improve your experience with SAP PaPM Cloud Standard Model. Stay tuned for more updates and innovations in the coming months.

Until next time! 😊

entry.title: Work Zone advanced Edition cdm_export_service
entry.content:
We would like to extract information from the Content Cards in BTP Work Zone Advanced Edition via cdm_export_service/v1/export/Entities. For example, we are trying to download the ZIP file via the portal service using this body:
{
‘entityRefs’: [
{ ‘ID’: ‘dws-widget-mr923ntn’, “Type”: ‘catalog’ }
]
}
Unfortunately, we only receive an Error.json file containing the following information:
[{‘detailedErrorID’:301,‘errorType’:‘General’,‘errorMessage’:‘Request failed with status code 500’,‘agentId’:‘content-repository’,“correlationId”:‘c9d732e3-0d30-42ae-5d0a-1cbb142679d8’}]
Are we doing something wrong, or is this simply not possible in the Advanced Edition?
Thanks for your help.
Jens

Translated with DeepL.com (free version)

entry.title: SAP Academic Community Conference NA 2026 - Digital Hub
entry.content:
We are excited to welcome you to the SAP ACC NA Digital Hub - your dedicated space to connect, collaborate, and continue the conversation throughout and beyond the conference.

This Digital Hub is designed to help you get the most out of your SAP ACC NA experience by providing a place where attendees, speakers, and SAP experts can engage, share insights, and learn from one another.

How to Connect and Engage

We encourage you to use this space to:

  • Ask follow-up questions about conference sessions and topics
  • Continue discussions with speakers and fellow participants
  • Share your experiences, ideas, and best practices
  • Exchange teaching and learning insights with peers

Don't Miss Any Discussions – Subscribe to the Label

To stay informed about all SAP ACC NA Digital Hub activities, we recommend that you:

Subscribe to the label "SAP ACC NA Digital Hub"

 

By subscribing, you'll receive notifications whenever new discussions and content are published under this label.

Creating a New Discussion

When posting a new discussion related to SAP ACC NA, please make sure to:

Add the label "SAP ACC NA Digital Hub" to your post

Using the label helps community members easily find all conference-related discussions in one place and ensures your post reaches others who are following the Digital Hub.

Explore and Contribute

The Digital Hub is designed to support ongoing collaboration within our community. Whether you want to reconnect with someone you met during the conference, seek advice from experts, or continue exploring a topic that inspired you, this is the place to do it.

Earn Your SAP ACC Digital Badge!

Don't forget that you can also earn your SAP ACC Digital Badge. Simply visit the dedicated blog post and leave a reply indicating whether you participated as an attendee or a speaker.

We look forward to your questions, ideas, and discussions. Let's make this a collaborative, engaging, and insightful experience for everyone!

And if not done yet, remember to join the SAP University Alliances program group to get the most out of your experience!
